turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: We'll hear arguments next in Heffron v. International Society for Krishna Consciousness. Mr. Harbison.

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor. however, also enjoined the Krishnas themselves from selling any of these items that I've mentioned on a peripatetic basis throughout the fairgrounds. In other words, the effect of that restraining order was to say, the state fair cannot require these respondents to engage in these activities only at a

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: Your Honor, they did not have a booth and the record doesn't indicate whether or not they solicited during the '78 or '79 or '80 fairs, in part because the record, the trial court record was closed at that time.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Harbison, following up on Justice Blackmun's question, at pages 35 and 36 of your brief, at the bottom of the page you make the statement, "in fact, even under current state fair policy, Krishnas could proselytize among and speak with fairgoers and then direct interested donors that's somehow a change of policy. That's the way it has al­ ways been under the booth rule.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That is, other fairgoers could -- ?

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: Anyone, Your Honor. In fact --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or anybody inside the premises could try tc convince anybody else of the wisdom of his religious or other ideological beliefs and direct such a person to the Krishna booth?

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: Exactly right, Your Honor. In fact,

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: And part of the reason as indicated in this case, the record shows that with respect to these respondents -- and I think they indicate this in paragraph 11 of the stipulation -- that they don't merely want to just dis­ tribute the flowers and the books and the magazines and the thousand people. There are in an average year more than 1.3 million people who attend this 12-day event and I emphasize it's they are or at least gives them some idea of the nature of the booth operation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When you speak of the booths, are you in­ cluding under that term the halls and areas where they have farm machinery and other machinery?

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. The booth rule in a sense is somewhat of a misnomer in that we're not talking only about little stands and booths but I am using the term to mean fixed locations generally. Sometimes it's Inside buildings, sometimes it's a location known as Machinery Hill that perhaps include charitable and noncommercial organizations as well. I think it's likely that a significant number, in addition to these, of the 1,400 booth operators would qualify under that definition, whatever the definition may be. But the rule, again, doesn't have any effect whatso­ ever on the ability or the right of anyone to engage in some communications or proselytizing, however you want to phrase it. It relates primarily to the distribution and the monetary ac­ tivities. The respondents in this case are a religious organi­ zation whose members engage in an activity known as Sankirtan. That consists of a number of aspects, singing, dancing, chant­ do that, I think. And not only so much because of the booth rule but because of the state fair management's inherent respon­ sibility, as this Court recognized in Cox v. New Hampshire, which involved public streets, the responsibility to maintain some order so that they're not overlapping parades, if you will. And I suggest that in this case there is even more evidence thar there was in Cox v. New Hampshire that that sort of conflict would be posed. The state fair would have a right and an obli­ gation, in fact, to the public to control that sort of activity. It doesn't mean that it couldn't occur. It just means it would have to be done in an orderly fashion so that there would be this five or six-day period when these sales and solicitations were taking place, the Krishnas represented -- I should say, mis' represented themselves to the potential donors and purchasers, indicating that they represented something called the Division of Natural Resources, and schools for needy and handicapped children, drug treatment programs, and indeed the state fair it­ self. And there is also evidence that these sorts of things took place with respect to donors who were minors. It's been suggested by the respondents that this booth rule is a totally preclusive regulation, and it strikes me that if that were the case it would be virtually Impossible ever to beliefs. And as I said, it doesn't prohibit any sort of oral proselytizing or oral conversations at all. One of the things at this stage of the case that I think is critical is that the respondents have now abandoned what I believe to be the basis, the key basis, for the Minne­ sota Supreme Court's decision. That is, they're no longer making a claim that they are entitled to a single exemption from the booth rule because of some unique status that they have because of this practice of Sankirtan.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean they've abandoned the Sankirtan

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or a candidate for a political office, I suppose.

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: I would think so, Your Honor. I don't see how the free exercise rights could be elevated over free speech or free association rights.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do the candidates and the parties have booths ?

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, they do. There are on fraudulent practices. Because if you look at the record, and even the stipulation, again, in paragraph 11, the Respon­ dents don't merely want to just distribute the books or the flowers or the incense or the flags. They want to get money for them. Now, whether that call that a sale or a donation, it's not just a pure distribution activity.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I don't quite -- how does that make

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: Because of the fact that there is grave if deception were absolutely prohibited at state fairs.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: P. T. Barnum wouldn't be the only one.

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: We wouldn't have egresses, I suppose, Your Honor. Well, I think -- we know that, we recognize that, even, it seems to me, under this Court's decisions such as -in Broadrick, even in First Amendment cases, that should not auto­ matically be applied and in fact, I think, the phrase, "sub­ stantial" --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, let's assume for the moment -- I mean, because of the fact that someone is doing something unique on the fairgrounds, especially giving something away free, I think that's going to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's a card that says, vote for me for county assessor, or something, but it's a --

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: Your Honor, but my point would be, witl respect to that, that those fairgoers may not know that right of a sudden the crowd becomes aware of the fact that dozens of Stevens, I gather, is you justify it on the basis of crowd con­ trol or traffic control. Is that it?

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. It is also related, I would think, to the protection of the fairgoers' rights of privacy themselves, particularly when we're talking about the any consequence? MR, HARBISON: This is, Your Honor. The record is pretty much silent on that but to my knowledge the state fair does not even receive a legislative appropriation from the State of Minnesota. It's a self -- constitutionally suspect because they would turn what is now a a non-content-directed rule into a regulation that inherently is going to require the state fair to look into the content and the religious basis of the organizations. They're suggesting that the state fair could do this by registering all organiza­ tions who are charitable or noncommercial, whomever they may be. The problem with that is, how does the fair determine The state in this case seeks to expel from the open and public areas of the state fair and to corral in these fixed booths, which indeed, as Mr. Harbison points out, are sometimes inside buildings, exercises of speech, press, and religion, which clearly lie at the very core of the First Amendment. directed to Mr. Harbison expose in a way that perhaps the tically the sweep and breadth of this rule. Mr. Justice White activity that is hardly at the periphery of the First Amendment. We're dealing here with core First Amendment activity, distri­ buting literature, and the fact that the law does reach that far is- one of the reasons that this Court should strike it down.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But aren't there going to be a large number of people, as suggested in Justice Todd's dissent, at A-73 of the Joint Appendix, who are going to be able to make the same claim as your clients, that they should be able to freely roam presumably all of them would do the same thing your clients have done.

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: I'm afraid, Mr. Justice Rehnquist, I can't share that presumption. The business of wandering around in the heat of a Minnesota State Fair hoping to attract attention is not an obviously pleasant alternative to a nicely rented, probably air-conditioned booth.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought that Minnesota was a cool state.

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: Well, I guess that the decision will not the arrival, chronologically, with the -- ?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: The language of the rule, we agree, pre­ dates their arrival, but it's interesting to note that the rule on its face says nothing about solicitation. It's simply been interpreted to encompass solicitation now. And the record doesn't itself illuminate the question of why the rule has been broadened to deal with solicitation. In light of the problem you are urging?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: I am certain you could, Mr. Chief Justice. And if you added political and charitable organizations, there might be more still. But even if you take the Attorney Gene­ ral's representation and attempt to paint the chaos at its worst, that perhaps there would be a thousand proselytizers and solicitors and distributors in an area where there are over 115,000 visitors a day, even if we assume at worst that one out of 100 people is trying to persuade someone else to give money in state fair after state fair where the lower courts have ruled at the behest of the Krishna Consciousness group that it was unconstitutional to ban solicitation and distribution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What about the New York district court ruling?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: Well, it is true that the New York dis­ trict court ruling now pending in this Court seeking certiorari before judgment has gone the other way, but in the majority of cases, in the 1+th Circuit',1 in the 7th Circuit,'in the Northern appropriate place for the exchange of ideas than streets and parks where people usually go to get someplace or to enjoy themselves. There is no suggestion that it is less appropriate in any sense than the other places that this Court has suggested are public fora. And in this kind of public forum to have a answer's no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How about out in the halls, where we have exhibits, and the museum downstairs?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: I think the quiet and tranquillity and the educational function of these halls is incompatible with that kind of wandering, although I must admit I haven't careful­ ly enough thought about it to want to be held to that. A state fair is at the other end of the spectrum, surely. missionary be any more or less a problem downstairs than at the state fair?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: Well, I suppose, Mr. Chief Justice, that because people come to this Court for a much more specific purpose and with a particular event or set of events in mind, certiorari.

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: Well, that's -- certainly, Mr. Justice. And the only kind of deference that I would suggest even re­ motely to that court's decision in this case is that its proxi­ mity to the situation and its sensitivity to the purposes that the State of Minnesota seeks to advance are at least as likely to be reflected in its judgment as this Court indicated was true in Michael M. v. Superior Court of Sonoma County, not long ago, when deference was to the Supreme Court of California is helpful. It makes it less problematic than it otherwise might for this Court to speculate on the purposes of the rule and the way in which it operates. Now, as to the way in which the rule --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Tribe, I think a minute ago you con­ ceded that a reasonable limitation on numbers would be consti­ tutional?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or in somebody else's -- or in the queues lined up at somebody else's booth?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: I think that probably would be all right, Mr. Justice White.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, certainly, they can be heard and seer in a booth that they have.

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: They can be heard and seen, but that's an important, I think, additional point, and that is, we suggested that the primary operation of the booth rule, since it's so

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't there another response to that argu­ ment, and that is that they do have the opportunity to try to persuade orally the people to go over to the booth?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: To some extent. But if they persuade too effectively, might they not be guilty of soliciting? That is, as this court thought in Thomas v. Collins, there may not be any very bright line between saying the union is your only real

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought it was clear -- maybe I missed not banning that kind of advocacy. That is, as the court below in the dissenting opinion, parts of --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the purpose is you confine the ex­ change of money to booths.

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: It's not just the exchange of money. solicitation

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: On page 35 and 36 of his brief he says, "In fact, even under current state fair policy, Krishnas could proselytize among and speak with fairgoers and then direct interested donors or purchasers to their booth."

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: That's correct, Mr. Justice Rehnquist. But what I'd be curious to know is whether the Attorney General

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: The stipulated facts say that soliciting in the fairgrounds is forbidden as well as --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But not soliciting for membership?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: Soliciting for contributions, not member ship.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you can solicit for support?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: Apparently. Although the line between vague. thoroughfares of a state fair is rather hard to believe; and moreover, the notion that it is permissible to limit donations to a religion to those people who are willing not only to make the donation but walk a mile in order to make it; that is, go all the way over to the booth and give their 25 cents or one dollar. It's also incompatible with the protected nature of the right to solicit donations. It's as though we were to tell could only take pledges for remote contributions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Were you denied -- did you request and were denied that opportunity?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: No, we think that the issue is one of the facial validity of this rule, and we think it would be far more pernicious to sacrifice the principle that the validity of the rule cannot be sustained by a few episodes, even if true, of misconduct, than to get involved in a sideshow of disputing we're all protected by the First Amendment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: But the distinction this Court drew in Village of Schaumberg v. Citizens for a Better Environment between the category of activities as applied to which the 75 percent rule in that case was unconstitutional, and other kinds of activities, raising money for more conventional be unconstitutional, is exactly the same kind of distinction as

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And therefore what?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: And therefore, that it was not valid --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't your overbreadth argument, though, isn't the bottom line that it's unconstitutional on its face and that if they want to apply it to a permissible category of activity, they should redraw it? against these litigants.

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: That's right. They did not strike it down on a ground as broad as we think would be appropriate for this Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, even under their holding I don't think the candidate for political office could get the benefit of this rule.

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: Well, the dissenting justices didn't quite agree. Justice Todd, joined by two other members of that court, said that although the majority might not have adverted to it,

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: Insofar as that made it easier to decide the case. What we are --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What I'm saying is, they clearly have not decided anything other than the fact that these litigants are entitled to relief.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why are you entitled to urge us to declare it unconstitutional on its face?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: As long as it's unconstitutional as to you?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: I suppose that It would be an ironic --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Overbreadth usually applies when it's not unconstitutional as to you but it may be as to others, doesn't it?

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: Well, I suppose the answer is that we are arguing that whether or not all of the activity in which respondents have engaged and seek to engage come within the protected core, we are entitled to have the rule struck down in­

turns.name: TRIBE
turns.text: They would much rather do more.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't it part of their religious mission to get the money at the time --

turns.name: HARBISON
turns.text: I'd like to take up, may it please the Court, on that very point. Under Minnesota statutes, every law in Minnesota is severable unless it's expressly provided not to be. Further­ more, the comment that the Minnesota Supreme Court suggested some lesser restrictive alternatives to the rule has to be taker with the understanding that their decision was based upon the quoted and it refers to "sale or distribution of any merchandise, including printed or written material, except under license issued for /_from/ the society and/or from a duly licensed loca­ tion." Now, as I read that, it does contemplate some licenses for distribution that are roving licenses, in effect. Is that right?
